romania.summary: O pacientă în vârstă de 50 de ani, diagnosticată cu colangiocarcinom intrahepatic (tumoră primară a ficatului), pentru care nu fusese identificată o soluție chirurgicală în România, a beneficiat de o intervenție de înaltă complexitate la Hautepierre Hospital din Strasbourg. Parcursul terapeutic a fost coordonat de Dr. Augustin Dima, medic primar chirurgie generală, cu supraspecializare în …

romania.summary: Gara Progresul din Capitalǎ va fi demolatǎ. Va fi inlocuitǎ cu o garǎ nouă, o stație de metrou a magistralei 4 de metrou și o parcare. Gara va deservi atât magistrala 4 de metrou, cât și linia feroviară București Progresul- Giurgiu, care va fi modernizată, inclusiv electificatǎ. Gara are o istorie de 60 de ani. …

romania.title: PWN România (Professional Women’s Network Romania) a aniversat împlinirea a 15 ani de activitate în România și deschide un nou capitol, cu un nou Consiliu Director
romania.summary: PWN România a reunit aproximativ 250 de invitați la evenimentul anual și aniversar de vară, intitulat „Garden of Growth”, și pregătește, din toamnă, relansarea programelor dedicate antreprenoriatului, mentoratului și reprezentării… Articolul PWN România (Professional Women’s Network Romania) a aniversat împlinirea a 15 ani de activitate în România și deschide un nou capitol, cu un nou Consiliu Director apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Timișoara: Investiții la Spitalul Clinic de Urgenta pentru Copii “Louis Turcanu” pentru creșterea capacității și dotarea infrastructurii spitalicești dedicate îngrijirii pacientului critic neonatal
romania.summary: Spitalul Clinic de Urgenta pentru Copii “Louis Turcanu” Timișoara anunță finalizarea proiectului cu titlul: “Șansa la viață prin creșterea capacității și dotarea infrastructurii spitalicești dedicate îngrijirii pacientului critic neonatal la… Articolul Timișoara: Investiții la Spitalul Clinic de Urgenta pentru Copii “Louis Turcanu” pentru creșterea capacității și dotarea infrastructurii spitalicești dedicate îngrijirii pacientului critic neonatal apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: „Gentle Monster”, cu Léa Seydoux și Catherine Deneuve, deschide ANONIMUL 2026
romania.summary: Cea de-a 23-a ediție a Festivalului Internațional de Film Independent ANONIMUL va fi deschisă de filmul „Gentle Monster”, un studiu psihologic intens despre încredere, memorie și destrămarea unei familii confruntate… Articolul „Gentle Monster”, cu Léa Seydoux și Catherine Deneuve, deschide ANONIMUL 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Peste 1.100 de persoane implicate în prevenirea bullying-ului și cyberbullying-ului în județul Alba
romania.summary: Asociația Centrul de Dezvoltare Personală și Combatere a Comportamentelor de Risc (PSIHO KIT) anunță finalizarea proiectului „Click pe Educație”, implementat în perioada 1 februarie – 31 iulie 2026, în cadrul… Articolul Peste 1.100 de persoane implicate în prevenirea bullying-ului și cyberbullying-ului în județul Alba apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Modulul FNT Educațional, ediția a 5-a. Spațiu esențial de expunere a procesului de educație prin teatru
romania.summary: Inițiat în anul 2022, modulul FNT Educațional continuă și anul acesta, fiind un pilon reprezentativ pentru direcția pedagogică a Festivalului Național de Teatru, o direcție mai importantă astăzi decât oricând.… Articolul Modulul FNT Educațional, ediția a 5-a. Spațiu esențial de expunere a procesului de educație prin teatru apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Acesta a recunoscut că filmul e bazat pe povestea reală a lui Codruţ, un prieten al lui Nolan care locuia în Rahova şi mergea la muncă tocmai în Pipera. „Am încercat să transpunem în imagini o […] The post Nolan: Filmul „Odiseea”, bazat pe povestea reală a unui om care locuieşte în Rahova şi lucrează în Pipera appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Deşi nimeni nu prea l-a băgat în seamă în cei patru ani de liceu, tânărul Aureliu Poplică din Brăila s-a trezit brusc vedeta generaţiei sale! Vestea că acesta a terminat liceul cu 10 pe linie – şi a mai luat şi 10 pe linie la BAC – s-a răspândit ca focul, iar mulţi foşti colegi […] The post Elev cu 10 pe linie, admirat de colegii de generaţie: „A tras 10 linii şi încă e în picioare? Wow!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj
romania.summary: Se pare că i-a ieşit combinaţia! Surse din zona de securitate cibernetică spun că temutul hacker care a atacat infrastructura IT a Agenţiei Naţionale de Cadastru a reuşit să îşi treacă în proprietate o locuinţă de peste 11 metri pătraţi într-o zonă destul de umană a municipiului Cluj. „Având acces la baza de date a […] The post S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Schimbarea educației din România de jos în sus, prin pregătirea generației a 13-a de profesori-soluție pentru școli din mediul rural.
romania.summary: București, 17 iulie 2026 – Teach for Romania a dat startul Academiei de Leadership și Formare în Educație, programul intensiv prin care pregătește cea de-a 13-a generație de viitori lideri în educație – directori, profesori, învățători și educatori, care să predea, începând cu anul școlar 2026-2027, și să transforme educația de la firul ierbii, în […]

romania.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: How the heart's 'little brain' helps it function and protects it against stress
medical.summary: For years, scientists have known that the heart has its own network of nerves, the intrinsic cardiac nervous system (ICNS), sometimes called its "little brain." Exactly how it functions has remained something of a mystery, but a new paper published in the journal Cell sheds light on how these heart nerves work to keep the heart beating steadily.

medical.title: New immune mechanism helps explain why ovarian cancer stops responding to chemotherapy
medical.summary: Scientists at The Wistar Institute have identified a new mechanism behind the chemotherapy resistance that makes ovarian cancer so lethal. In a new study published in The Journal for ImmunoTherapy of Cancer, the researchers show that chemotherapy triggers an inflammatory cascade that recruits immune cells into the tumor microenvironment, which ultimately function to protect the cancer from subsequent chemotherapy.

medical.title: Test offers new way to personalize treatment, reduce unnecessary chemotherapy for patients with breast cancer
medical.summary: A new cancer diagnostic test marketed by Veracyte and developed by a group of researchers, including UNC geneticist Charles Perou, Ph.D., has been found to accurately predict whether patients with breast cancer need chemotherapy or whether hormone therapy alone can safely and effectively lead to remission.

medical.title: Could increasing omega-3 intake boost recovery and long-term health in elite athletes?
medical.summary: As the Commonwealth Games approach, athletes chasing every extra edge on the track, court or in the pool might not find it in training, but on their dinner plates. In a study published in Sports Medicine—Open, University of Wollongong (UOW) researchers analyzed the diets and omega-3 blood levels of professional athletes, finding that almost three-quarters could benefit from increasing their intake of these healthy fats.

medical.title: How telehealth is fueling a surge in ADHD scripts in a business model that benefits the rich
medical.summary: Seeing a psychiatrist via telehealth became easier from January 2022. That's when Medicare-reimbursed telepsychiatry arrangements became permanent.

medical.title: Study finds malaria chemoprevention for school-aged children could deliver billions in health and economic benefits
medical.summary: Providing malaria chemoprevention to school-age children in some of Africa's highest-burden countries could generate billions of dollars in societal benefits while improving health and education outcomes, according to a new study led by researchers at LSTM.

medical.title: Study uses virtual reality to improve walking balance assessment
medical.summary: A new study led by Clarkson University Associate Professor of Physical Therapy Kwadwo Appiah-Kubi introduces a virtual reality tool that could help clinicians better evaluate walking balance and detect balance problems before they lead to falls.

medical.title: A patient-focused health care system is key to tackling missingness
medical.summary: Embedding a patient-focused approach across all health care settings could help better address the problem of missingness in health care, according to researchers.

medical.title: Popular childhood drinks may raise blood pressure decades later
medical.summary: A 25-year study found that frequent consumption of soda, sports drinks, and fruit juice beginning in childhood was linked to a higher risk of high blood pressure in adulthood. Swapping one daily drink for whole fruit, milk, or water could significantly reduce that risk.

medical.title: Goodbye, cavities? New gel could regrow tooth enamel
medical.summary: Researchers have developed a fluoride-free gel that could help rebuild damaged tooth enamel, something the body cannot naturally replace once it is lost. Inspired by the proteins that guide enamel formation in infancy, the gel fills microscopic cracks and draws calcium and phosphate from saliva to grow a new mineral layer that closely matches healthy enamel.

medical.title: Natural compound may fight rheumatoid arthritis at its source
medical.summary: A plant-derived compound reduced swelling, inflammation, and joint damage in rats with rheumatoid arthritis. It also helped rebalance immune activity and improve the way certain fats were processed in the body. Researchers say the findings could lead to a new approach for treating the disease.

medical.title: Scientists discover the hidden brain switch that assigns bees their jobs
medical.summary: Researchers found that specific brain circuits help decide which jobs bees perform as they age. When those circuits were switched off, older worker bees began caring for the queen again instead of carrying out their normal later-life duties. The findings reveal that a bee colony’s complex division of labor may be guided by neural switches hidden inside each bee’s brain.

medical.title: ‘It’s brought a much-needed sense of community back to the town’
medical.summary: A growing backlash against the Premier League has sparked huge interest in community clubs, which make football affordable for everyone The post ‘It’s brought a much-needed sense of community back to the town’ appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Popular sugar substitutes linked to faster brain aging
medical.summary: Several popular sugar substitutes may not be as harmless as they seem. Adults who consumed the most artificial sweeteners showed substantially faster declines in memory and thinking, especially if they were under 60 or had diabetes. The highest intake was linked to cognitive aging roughly 1.6 years faster than the lowest intake. Researchers stressed that more studies are needed before concluding that sweeteners are the cause.

medical.title: Intermittent fasting helped people keep weight off for a year
medical.summary: A 12-week intermittent fasting program produced weight-loss benefits that were still visible a year later. Participants who ate within an eight-hour window maintained more weight loss than those who followed their usual longer eating schedule. Both early and late eating windows worked, while early fasting appeared especially helpful for preserving fat loss.

medical.title: Mediterranean diet may activate tiny proteins that protect the heart and brain
medical.summary: The Mediterranean diet may influence aging through tiny proteins produced inside the cell’s mitochondria. Older adults who followed the diet most closely had higher levels of humanin and SHMOOSE, which have been linked to heart and brain protection. Olive oil, fish, legumes, and fewer refined carbohydrates appeared especially important. The findings could eventually help scientists develop more personalized nutrition plans for healthy aging.

medical.title: Your fingers may hold a secret of human brain evolution
medical.summary: A study of 225 newborns suggests prenatal estrogen may have played a role in the evolution of larger human brains. Boys with finger-length patterns linked to higher estrogen exposure before birth tended to have larger head circumferences, which are strongly associated with brain size. The same connection was not seen in girls.

medical.title: Why are healthy young non-smokers developing lung cancer?
medical.summary: An unexpected study found that young non-smokers with healthier diets had higher rates of lung cancer, raising questions about whether pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could play a role. Researchers stress that the findings are preliminary and require further studies before any conclusions can be drawn.

medical.title: Where you live could shape your dementia risk, massive study finds
medical.summary: Researchers analyzing data from more than 214,000 people found that dementia risk factors differ widely across countries, challenging the idea of a one-size-fits-all prevention strategy. At the same time, they uncovered surprisingly consistent patterns that could help shape smarter, more targeted public health efforts.

science.title: Would you trust AI for ethical advice?
science.summary: Most people would not prefer to take ethical advice from a computer, but a new Wharton study shows how attitudes change when users see how good AI guidance can be.

science.title: Moon baboon: New insight into the mind of ancient Egyptians
science.summary: New research from the University of St Andrews has shed light on baboons, moonlight and their link to an ancient Egyptian deity.

science.title: From one frontier to another: The quantum revolution
science.summary: Manchester's quantum researchers are building on the Ferranti Mark I legacy, using ultra-pure silicon and single atoms to move quantum computing closer to real-world impact.

science.title: How much should you reveal about yourself on your dating profile? Scientists have found the 'sweet spot'
science.summary: Dating has entered a new era in which finding a potential soulmate is increasingly shaped by the digital world and, often, by algorithms. On dating apps, the way people present themselves through their profiles can significantly influence how approachable, trustworthy and likable they appear.

science.title: Shaking atoms to bring black-hole quantum chaos into the lab
science.summary: Physicists have discovered a surprisingly simple way to reproduce one of the most fascinating models in modern physics—linked to black holes, quantum chaos and exotic electronic materials—using ultracold atoms trapped in light.

science.title: Molecular clock transitions tune out the noise in the hunt for new physics
science.summary: Heavy polar molecules are some of the most sensitive tools physicists have for probing what lies beyond the Standard Model, the theory that describes the particles and forces we know about. But turning that sensitivity into precise, trustworthy measurements has long been held back by one stubborn problem: Stray electric and magnetic fields drown out the tiny signals researchers are actually looking for.

science.title: NASA Astronaut Chris Williams Closes Out Space Station Mission
science.summary: After eight months aboard the International Space Station for his first mission, NASA astronaut Chris Williams is preparing to return to Earth. During his assignment, Williams contributed to research for new cancer treatments, advanced the production of materials to improve computers and electronics, ventured into the vacuum of space to complete two spacewalks, and much […]

science.title: Fig-leaf enzyme turns tannery waste into high-value collagen
science.summary: Tanneries generate large quantities of skin trimmings before hides are tanned. These trimmings contain collagen but are commonly treated as low-value waste. An international research team has developed a method that uses ficin, a protein-digesting enzyme obtained from fig leaves, to recover type I collagen from lambskin trimming waste.

science.title: Older teens entering care often overlooked
science.summary: There is an urgent need for better support for older teenagers entering care for the first time, new Griffith University research has found. Professor Silke Meyer from Griffith's School of Allied Health, Sport and Social Work conducted a scoping review that examined research on older adolescents ages 15–17.

science.title: New teaching method could help Aboriginal languages survive and thrive
science.summary: Curtin University-led research has unveiled a new teaching approach that could help incorporate Aboriginal languages into Australian classrooms, teach younger generations and potentially prevent important cultural connections from being lost.

science.title: NASA will test SpaceX and Blue Origin Moon landers before the next lunar landing
science.summary: NASA is preparing for future astronaut landings on the Moon with an unusually ambitious orbital rehearsal. During Artemis III in 2027, Orion astronauts will rendezvous and dock separately with prototype lunar landers from Blue Origin and SpaceX, allowing crews and ground teams to test the complex maneuvers needed for later missions.

science.title: NASA’s Webb telescope discovers giant planet hiding in one of astronomy’s most famous systems
science.summary: A giant planet had been hiding inside one of astronomy’s most closely studied planetary systems, concealed by a bright disk of cosmic dust. Webb revealed Beta Pictoris d through atmospheric traces of carbon monoxide, water vapor, and methane, showcasing a promising new method for finding elusive exoplanets.

science.title: NASA’s James Webb catches a supermassive black hole feeding
science.summary: JWST has captured unusually detailed images of gas feeding the supermassive black hole at the center of NGC 4696. A vast filament appears to funnel material into an 800-light-year-wide spinning disk, where gas races around at up to 600 kilometers per second. The findings suggest black holes may recycle their own fuel by heating gas with jets and later drawing the cooled material back in.

science.title: Terraforming Mars may be more realistic than scientists once thought
science.summary: Scientists say new technologies have reopened the debate over whether Mars could someday be terraformed, turning a once impossible idea into a serious research topic. Before anyone tries to reshape the Red Planet, though, researchers say we must understand the risks, including what might be lost if Mars already harbors its own forms of life.

science.title: “Silly sprinklers” help scientists finally solve Feynman’s famous sprinkler mystery
science.summary: A team of mathematicians used whimsical "silly sprinklers" to solve a physics mystery that has puzzled scientists for decades. Their experiments showed that the rotation of both normal and reverse sprinklers is driven by the momentum of flowing water, not by the outside water flow or other long-standing theories. The results finally provide a clear answer to Feynman’s famous sprinkler problem. They could also help engineers design more efficient fluid-powered machines.

science.title: Koalas nearly went extinct before humans arrived, DNA study reveals
science.summary: A major DNA study has rewritten the koala's evolutionary story, revealing that the species suffered a dramatic population collapse about 100,000 years ago, long before humans arrived in Australia. By calculating the koala's mutation rate for the first time and analyzing hundreds of genomes, researchers discovered that every living koala traces back to a small group that survived extreme climate shifts and later repopulated eastern Australia.

science.title: A 200-year-old physics experiment could help build future computers
science.summary: Scientists at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore have discovered a surprisingly simple way to create exotic light structures called optical skyrmions using a 200-year-old optical effect known as the Poisson spot. Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered materials, they simply shine a laser at a tiny circular disc, producing stable swirling patterns in light that researchers believe could one day help power advanced data storage, communications, and computing technologies.

science.title: Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade
science.summary: Scientists have developed a new framework that could finally apply the laws of thermodynamics to real, ever-changing black holes instead of only perfectly stable ones. The advance may improve our understanding of black hole mergers, evaporation, and the powerful gravitational wave events detected by observatories like LIGO.

science.title: Physicists say quantum mechanics may not need imaginary numbers after all
science.summary: Physicists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) have examined a fundamental property of quantum mechanics in collaboration with the German Aerospace Center (DLR). In the scientific journal Physical Review Letters, they show that this theory does not necessarily need to be formulated with imaginary numbers – real numbers can in fact also be used. The American Physical Society has also dedicated a “Highlight” to these findings in its Physics Magazine.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: 😂 Nolan: Filmul „Odiseea”, bazat pe povestea reală a unui om care locuieşte în Rahova şi lucrează în Pipera
mix_items.summary: Regizorul şi scenaristul Christopher Nolan a confirmat aseară toate bănuielile românilor în ceea ce priveşte ultimul său blockbuster, „Odiseea”! Acesta a recunoscut că filmul e bazat pe povestea reală a lui Codruţ, un prieten al lui Nolan care locuia în Rahova şi mergea la muncă tocmai în Pipera. „Am încercat să transpunem în imagini o […] The post Nolan: Filmul „Odiseea”, bazat pe povestea reală a unui om care locuieşte în Rahova şi lucrează în Pipera appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Peste 1.100 de persoane implicate în prevenirea bullying-ului și cyberbullying-ului în județul Alba
mix_items.summary: Asociația Centrul de Dezvoltare Personală și Combatere a Comportamentelor de Risc (PSIHO KIT) anunță finalizarea proiectului „Click pe Educație”, implementat în perioada 1 februarie – 31 iulie 2026, în cadrul… Articolul Peste 1.100 de persoane implicate în prevenirea bullying-ului și cyberbullying-ului în județul Alba apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Modulul FNT Educațional, ediția a 5-a. Spațiu esențial de expunere a procesului de educație prin teatru
mix_items.summary: Inițiat în anul 2022, modulul FNT Educațional continuă și anul acesta, fiind un pilon reprezentativ pentru direcția pedagogică a Festivalului Național de Teatru, o direcție mai importantă astăzi decât oricând.… Articolul Modulul FNT Educațional, ediția a 5-a. Spațiu esențial de expunere a procesului de educație prin teatru apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Școli mai sigure pentru fiecare copil, prin educație și responsabilitate comună. Articolul Mediu școlar mai sigur pentru copii apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: „WEBTECTIVII”
mix_items.summary: Educație pentru siguranța copiilor în mediul online Articolul „WEBTECTIVII” apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Deşi nimeni nu prea l-a băgat în seamă în cei patru ani de liceu, tânărul Aureliu Poplică din Brăila s-a trezit brusc vedeta generaţiei sale! Vestea că acesta a terminat liceul cu 10 pe linie – şi a mai luat şi 10 pe linie la BAC – s-a răspândit ca focul, iar mulţi foşti colegi […] The post Elev cu 10 pe linie, admirat de colegii de generaţie: „A tras 10 linii şi încă e în picioare? Wow!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj
mix_items.summary: Se pare că i-a ieşit combinaţia! Surse din zona de securitate cibernetică spun că temutul hacker care a atacat infrastructura IT a Agenţiei Naţionale de Cadastru a reuşit să îşi treacă în proprietate o locuinţă de peste 11 metri pătraţi într-o zonă destul de umană a municipiului Cluj. „Având acces la baza de date a […] The post S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Schimbarea educației din România de jos în sus, prin pregătirea generației a 13-a de profesori-soluție pentru școli din mediul rural.
mix_items.summary: București, 17 iulie 2026 – Teach for Romania a dat startul Academiei de Leadership și Formare în Educație, programul intensiv prin care pregătește cea de-a 13-a generație de viitori lideri în educație – directori, profesori, învățători și educatori, care să predea, începând cu anul școlar 2026-2027, și să transforme educația de la firul ierbii, în […]

mix_items.title: Aproape 100.000 de copii învață în clase simultane. World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră.
mix_items.summary: București, 16 iulie. Aproape 100.000 de elevi din România învață în clase organizate în regim simultan, majoritatea în mediul rural,... Articolul Aproape 100.000 de copii învață în clase simultane. World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Would you trust AI for ethical advice?
mix_items.summary: Most people would not prefer to take ethical advice from a computer, but a new Wharton study shows how attitudes change when users see how good AI guidance can be.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Moon baboon: New insight into the mind of ancient Egyptians
mix_items.summary: New research from the University of St Andrews has shed light on baboons, moonlight and their link to an ancient Egyptian deity.

mix_items.title: From one frontier to another: The quantum revolution
mix_items.summary: Manchester's quantum researchers are building on the Ferranti Mark I legacy, using ultra-pure silicon and single atoms to move quantum computing closer to real-world impact.

mix_items.title: How much should you reveal about yourself on your dating profile? Scientists have found the 'sweet spot'
mix_items.summary: Dating has entered a new era in which finding a potential soulmate is increasingly shaped by the digital world and, often, by algorithms. On dating apps, the way people present themselves through their profiles can significantly influence how approachable, trustworthy and likable they appear.

mix_items.title: How the heart's 'little brain' helps it function and protects it against stress
mix_items.summary: For years, scientists have known that the heart has its own network of nerves, the intrinsic cardiac nervous system (ICNS), sometimes called its "little brain." Exactly how it functions has remained something of a mystery, but a new paper published in the journal Cell sheds light on how these heart nerves work to keep the heart beating steadily.

mix_items.title: New immune mechanism helps explain why ovarian cancer stops responding to chemotherapy
mix_items.summary: Scientists at The Wistar Institute have identified a new mechanism behind the chemotherapy resistance that makes ovarian cancer so lethal. In a new study published in The Journal for ImmunoTherapy of Cancer, the researchers show that chemotherapy triggers an inflammatory cascade that recruits immune cells into the tumor microenvironment, which ultimately function to protect the cancer from subsequent chemotherapy.

mix_items.title: Shaking atoms to bring black-hole quantum chaos into the lab
mix_items.summary: Physicists have discovered a surprisingly simple way to reproduce one of the most fascinating models in modern physics—linked to black holes, quantum chaos and exotic electronic materials—using ultracold atoms trapped in light.

mix_items.title: Test offers new way to personalize treatment, reduce unnecessary chemotherapy for patients with breast cancer
mix_items.summary: A new cancer diagnostic test marketed by Veracyte and developed by a group of researchers, including UNC geneticist Charles Perou, Ph.D., has been found to accurately predict whether patients with breast cancer need chemotherapy or whether hormone therapy alone can safely and effectively lead to remission.

mix_items.title: Could increasing omega-3 intake boost recovery and long-term health in elite athletes?
mix_items.summary: As the Commonwealth Games approach, athletes chasing every extra edge on the track, court or in the pool might not find it in training, but on their dinner plates. In a study published in Sports Medicine—Open, University of Wollongong (UOW) researchers analyzed the diets and omega-3 blood levels of professional athletes, finding that almost three-quarters could benefit from increasing their intake of these healthy fats.

mix_items.title: Molecular clock transitions tune out the noise in the hunt for new physics
mix_items.summary: Heavy polar molecules are some of the most sensitive tools physicists have for probing what lies beyond the Standard Model, the theory that describes the particles and forces we know about. But turning that sensitivity into precise, trustworthy measurements has long been held back by one stubborn problem: Stray electric and magnetic fields drown out the tiny signals researchers are actually looking for.

mix_items.title: NASA Astronaut Chris Williams Closes Out Space Station Mission
mix_items.summary: After eight months aboard the International Space Station for his first mission, NASA astronaut Chris Williams is preparing to return to Earth. During his assignment, Williams contributed to research for new cancer treatments, advanced the production of materials to improve computers and electronics, ventured into the vacuum of space to complete two spacewalks, and much […]

mix_items.title: How telehealth is fueling a surge in ADHD scripts in a business model that benefits the rich
mix_items.summary: Seeing a psychiatrist via telehealth became easier from January 2022. That's when Medicare-reimbursed telepsychiatry arrangements became permanent.

mix_items.title: Fig-leaf enzyme turns tannery waste into high-value collagen
mix_items.summary: Tanneries generate large quantities of skin trimmings before hides are tanned. These trimmings contain collagen but are commonly treated as low-value waste. An international research team has developed a method that uses ficin, a protein-digesting enzyme obtained from fig leaves, to recover type I collagen from lambskin trimming waste.

mix_items.title: Study finds malaria chemoprevention for school-aged children could deliver billions in health and economic benefits
mix_items.summary: Providing malaria chemoprevention to school-age children in some of Africa's highest-burden countries could generate billions of dollars in societal benefits while improving health and education outcomes, according to a new study led by researchers at LSTM.

mix_items.title: Older teens entering care often overlooked
mix_items.summary: There is an urgent need for better support for older teenagers entering care for the first time, new Griffith University research has found. Professor Silke Meyer from Griffith's School of Allied Health, Sport and Social Work conducted a scoping review that examined research on older adolescents ages 15–17.

mix_items.title: Study uses virtual reality to improve walking balance assessment
mix_items.summary: A new study led by Clarkson University Associate Professor of Physical Therapy Kwadwo Appiah-Kubi introduces a virtual reality tool that could help clinicians better evaluate walking balance and detect balance problems before they lead to falls.

mix_items.title: A patient-focused health care system is key to tackling missingness
mix_items.summary: Embedding a patient-focused approach across all health care settings could help better address the problem of missingness in health care, according to researchers.

mix_items.title: New teaching method could help Aboriginal languages survive and thrive
mix_items.summary: Curtin University-led research has unveiled a new teaching approach that could help incorporate Aboriginal languages into Australian classrooms, teach younger generations and potentially prevent important cultural connections from being lost.

mix_items.title: Popular childhood drinks may raise blood pressure decades later
mix_items.summary: A 25-year study found that frequent consumption of soda, sports drinks, and fruit juice beginning in childhood was linked to a higher risk of high blood pressure in adulthood. Swapping one daily drink for whole fruit, milk, or water could significantly reduce that risk.

mix_items.title: NASA will test SpaceX and Blue Origin Moon landers before the next lunar landing
mix_items.summary: NASA is preparing for future astronaut landings on the Moon with an unusually ambitious orbital rehearsal. During Artemis III in 2027, Orion astronauts will rendezvous and dock separately with prototype lunar landers from Blue Origin and SpaceX, allowing crews and ground teams to test the complex maneuvers needed for later missions.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Webb telescope discovers giant planet hiding in one of astronomy’s most famous systems
mix_items.summary: A giant planet had been hiding inside one of astronomy’s most closely studied planetary systems, concealed by a bright disk of cosmic dust. Webb revealed Beta Pictoris d through atmospheric traces of carbon monoxide, water vapor, and methane, showcasing a promising new method for finding elusive exoplanets.

mix_items.title: Goodbye, cavities? New gel could regrow tooth enamel
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed a fluoride-free gel that could help rebuild damaged tooth enamel, something the body cannot naturally replace once it is lost. Inspired by the proteins that guide enamel formation in infancy, the gel fills microscopic cracks and draws calcium and phosphate from saliva to grow a new mineral layer that closely matches healthy enamel.

mix_items.title: Natural compound may fight rheumatoid arthritis at its source
mix_items.summary: A plant-derived compound reduced swelling, inflammation, and joint damage in rats with rheumatoid arthritis. It also helped rebalance immune activity and improve the way certain fats were processed in the body. Researchers say the findings could lead to a new approach for treating the disease.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the hidden brain switch that assigns bees their jobs
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that specific brain circuits help decide which jobs bees perform as they age. When those circuits were switched off, older worker bees began caring for the queen again instead of carrying out their normal later-life duties. The findings reveal that a bee colony’s complex division of labor may be guided by neural switches hidden inside each bee’s brain.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: ‘It’s brought a much-needed sense of community back to the town’
mix_items.summary: A growing backlash against the Premier League has sparked huge interest in community clubs, which make football affordable for everyone The post ‘It’s brought a much-needed sense of community back to the town’ appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Popular sugar substitutes linked to faster brain aging
mix_items.summary: Several popular sugar substitutes may not be as harmless as they seem. Adults who consumed the most artificial sweeteners showed substantially faster declines in memory and thinking, especially if they were under 60 or had diabetes. The highest intake was linked to cognitive aging roughly 1.6 years faster than the lowest intake. Researchers stressed that more studies are needed before concluding that sweeteners are the cause.

mix_items.title: Intermittent fasting helped people keep weight off for a year
mix_items.summary: A 12-week intermittent fasting program produced weight-loss benefits that were still visible a year later. Participants who ate within an eight-hour window maintained more weight loss than those who followed their usual longer eating schedule. Both early and late eating windows worked, while early fasting appeared especially helpful for preserving fat loss.

mix_items.title: NASA’s James Webb catches a supermassive black hole feeding
mix_items.summary: JWST has captured unusually detailed images of gas feeding the supermassive black hole at the center of NGC 4696. A vast filament appears to funnel material into an 800-light-year-wide spinning disk, where gas races around at up to 600 kilometers per second. The findings suggest black holes may recycle their own fuel by heating gas with jets and later drawing the cooled material back in.

mix_items.title: Mediterranean diet may activate tiny proteins that protect the heart and brain
mix_items.summary: The Mediterranean diet may influence aging through tiny proteins produced inside the cell’s mitochondria. Older adults who followed the diet most closely had higher levels of humanin and SHMOOSE, which have been linked to heart and brain protection. Olive oil, fish, legumes, and fewer refined carbohydrates appeared especially important. The findings could eventually help scientists develop more personalized nutrition plans for healthy aging.

mix_items.title: Terraforming Mars may be more realistic than scientists once thought
mix_items.summary: Scientists say new technologies have reopened the debate over whether Mars could someday be terraformed, turning a once impossible idea into a serious research topic. Before anyone tries to reshape the Red Planet, though, researchers say we must understand the risks, including what might be lost if Mars already harbors its own forms of life.

mix_items.title: “Silly sprinklers” help scientists finally solve Feynman’s famous sprinkler mystery
mix_items.summary: A team of mathematicians used whimsical "silly sprinklers" to solve a physics mystery that has puzzled scientists for decades. Their experiments showed that the rotation of both normal and reverse sprinklers is driven by the momentum of flowing water, not by the outside water flow or other long-standing theories. The results finally provide a clear answer to Feynman’s famous sprinkler problem. They could also help engineers design more efficient fluid-powered machines.

mix_items.title: Your fingers may hold a secret of human brain evolution
mix_items.summary: A study of 225 newborns suggests prenatal estrogen may have played a role in the evolution of larger human brains. Boys with finger-length patterns linked to higher estrogen exposure before birth tended to have larger head circumferences, which are strongly associated with brain size. The same connection was not seen in girls.

mix_items.title: Koalas nearly went extinct before humans arrived, DNA study reveals
mix_items.summary: A major DNA study has rewritten the koala's evolutionary story, revealing that the species suffered a dramatic population collapse about 100,000 years ago, long before humans arrived in Australia. By calculating the koala's mutation rate for the first time and analyzing hundreds of genomes, researchers discovered that every living koala traces back to a small group that survived extreme climate shifts and later repopulated eastern Australia.

mix_items.title: A 200-year-old physics experiment could help build future computers
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore have discovered a surprisingly simple way to create exotic light structures called optical skyrmions using a 200-year-old optical effect known as the Poisson spot. Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered materials, they simply shine a laser at a tiny circular disc, producing stable swirling patterns in light that researchers believe could one day help power advanced data storage, communications, and computing technologies.

mix_items.title: Why are healthy young non-smokers developing lung cancer?
mix_items.summary: An unexpected study found that young non-smokers with healthier diets had higher rates of lung cancer, raising questions about whether pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could play a role. Researchers stress that the findings are preliminary and require further studies before any conclusions can be drawn.

mix_items.title: Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade
mix_items.summary: Scientists have developed a new framework that could finally apply the laws of thermodynamics to real, ever-changing black holes instead of only perfectly stable ones. The advance may improve our understanding of black hole mergers, evaporation, and the powerful gravitational wave events detected by observatories like LIGO.

mix_items.title: Physicists say quantum mechanics may not need imaginary numbers after all
mix_items.summary: Physicists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) have examined a fundamental property of quantum mechanics in collaboration with the German Aerospace Center (DLR). In the scientific journal Physical Review Letters, they show that this theory does not necessarily need to be formulated with imaginary numbers – real numbers can in fact also be used. The American Physical Society has also dedicated a “Highlight” to these findings in its Physics Magazine.

mix_items.title: Where you live could shape your dementia risk, massive study finds
mix_items.summary: Researchers analyzing data from more than 214,000 people found that dementia risk factors differ widely across countries, challenging the idea of a one-size-fits-all prevention strategy. At the same time, they uncovered surprisingly consistent patterns that could help shape smarter, more targeted public health efforts.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am facut pasi spre succes. Majoritatea au fost spre bucatarie.

satire_ro.title: S-a molipsit? De când s-a pupat cu un urmărit internaţional, Ciolacu dă şi el semne de urmărire internaţională
